dialogues: You just saw four rectangles and noticed differences between them. Things like how many rows they had or how long each row was. Now you're going to build your own rectangles on the grid, one row at a time. When you tap Check, you'll see how many squares you used. And we'll write a sentence that names what that area means. Everything we do today builds to one sentence: ___ is a multiple of ___ because ___ × ___ = ___. Let's build the first rectangle now.
You have 12 tiles. Place them on the grid to make a rectangle.
When your rectangle is complete, tap Check.
Nice. Your rectangle has tiles in rows.
Now fill in the sentence to describe what you built.
That's it. You built rows of equal length. That describes a rectangle.
Use the checked rectangle to fill the sentence. How many rows do you see? How many squares are in each row?
Right. You tied the built object to the sentence. Row count and row size are separate values.
Watch what happens to your rectangle. It's snapping into the grid. Same rectangle, easier to read.
Tap a highlighted row or side to show you see the rows.
Do you see the rows? Every row has the same number of squares.
Now build a rectangle where one side is 4 squares long. Drag the handle to extend the rectangle, then tap Check.
Right. Your rectangle is 4 wide and the height you chose. The area is shown at the center.
Now fill in the sentence about your rectangle.
Right. Your sentence says the area is a multiple of the fixed side length because width times height equals the area. That matches the rectangle you built.
You built one rectangle with that side length. Now build a different rectangle with the same side length. Drag the handle to a different height.
Now complete the sentence with this new area.
Right. You built two different rectangles with the same side length. Both areas are multiples of 4. Every time you add a row of 4, you get the next multiple.
